title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Welcome

AI is transforming the way coding is done
AI is rapidly evolving - tools and capability changing daily (this talk will probably be out of date by next week!)
Today I’ll focus on coding assistants I’ve personally used, but this is by no means exhaustive
Since tools are changing so rapidly, we’ll also talk about best practices
I’m no AI expert - but rather just an enthuastic (and optimistically skeptical) user
I also really want to hear from you

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
What exactly is Positron?

Developed by Posit team (formerly RStudio)
It is a modern, polygot successor to RStudio IDE
Built-in functionality for data science workflows
Current focus on R and Python
It is fundamentally a VS Code fork

Almost all VS Code extensions work in Positron
Positron has additional extensions as well

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: What exactly is a coding assistant?
text:
What exactly is a coding assistant?

Integrated directly into your IDE
Most have “system” state awareness (codebase, file structure, etc)
Some also have “live environment” state awareness (loaded data frames, plot pane, console history, etc)
Can operate in different modes: ask, edit, agent, or plan, depending on your needs and comfort level
Great for data science workflows (but anything really)
Often “BYO-key”: can use different LLM backends, such as Claude, GPT, etc.

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Coding agent options in Positron

Positron Databot

System state and live environment state awareness
BYOK (access to many LLM backends)

Positron Assistant

System state and live environment state awareness
BYOK (access to many LLM backends)

Claude Code

System state awareness
Access to Claude models

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: What LLM backend is best for coding?
text:
What LLM backend is best for coding?
Short answer: Claude (for R, at least for now…)

Check out Claude’s Constitution
Keep an eye on Posit’s AI Newsletter and R Benchmark tests

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Data privacy

Always check the data privacy policies of any AI tool you use!
If using an agent that is BYOK, you’re also subject to that backend’s data privacy policies
Claude Code has settings for data retention, whether your code is used to train models, etc

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Positron Databot

Developed by Posit team (formerly RStudio)
Allows you to interact with your data using natural language
Designed for exploratory data analysis (can do ML too)
Designed with responsible, human-in-the-loop use in mind (Databot is not a flotation device)
Uses a WEAR loop: Write code, Execute, Analyze, Regroup
BYOK: Can use various LLM backends (Claude, GPT, etc.)

“In my 30-year career writing software professionally, Databot is both the most exciting software I’ve worked on, and also the most dangerous.” –Joe Cheng, Posit CTO

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Positron Assistant

Developed by Posit team for Positron
General coding assistant for wide range of tasks
Similar to Claude Caude, but specifically tailored for data science workflows, with specific R and Python tooling
Can be used for code generation, debugging, documentation, and more
Aware of your “system” state: codebase, file structure, etc
Also aware of your “live” environment state: loaded data frames, plot pane, console history, etc
Can be used in ask, edit, agent, or plan mode
BYOK: Can use various LLM backends (Claude, GPT, etc.)

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: Which LLM backend for Positron Databot and Assistant: Claude or GitHub Copilot with Claude?
text:
Which LLM backend for Positron Databot and Assistant: Claude or GitHub Copilot with Claude?

Claude requires a paid account; Copilot has options
Copilot gives access to Claude models, but also others
Using Claude directly gives you access to full context window - it is capped when going through Copilot
Using Claude directly is faster
Using Claude through Copilot can quickly exhaust your Copliot credits (at least wotj the education account)

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Claude Code

Claude Code has a VS Code extenion, which is similar to Positron Assistant: both provide agentic capabilities
Assistant is tailored for data science with R and Python; Claude Code more towards general software engineering
Claude Code has great awareness of “system” state, but not as much “live” environment state awareness
Assistant is BYOK; Claude Code only uses Claude models

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: So how does billing for Claude work?
text:
So how does billing for Claude work?

Both Positon Assistant and Databot require an API key

Managed through Claude Developer Platform: https://platform.claude.com
Pay-as-you-go for tokens
API key support has been requested for monthly Claude Pro subscription, but not yet available

Claude Code requires a monthly subscription

$20/month for “Claude Pro”
Managed through https://claude.ai

These are two separate billing systems!

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
What about GitHub Copilot?

Can be used as an LLM backend for Positron Assistant and Databot, giving you access to Claude models (and others)
Can do in-like code suggestions
Can generate pull requests, commit messages, etc
Can review code and suggest improvements (either reviewing PRs, or even code before it’s committed!)
If you can do it on github.com, chances are you can probably do it directly inside Positron or VS Code
GitHub Copilot Pro has free educator accces for students and teachers (apply here)

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: Getting fancy: Customizing your AI coding assistants
text:
Getting fancy: Customizing your AI coding assistants

instructions.md: Specify custom “always-on” instructions: coding standards, style guides, etc to use across all scripts (specify the how) (also called claude.md or positron.md)
prompts.md: Define reusables prompts for tasks you commonly ask your assistant to do (e.g., “write a function that does X”, etc) (specify the what)
agents.md: Create custom agent personas that can perform specific skills (defined in skills.md), such as data cleaning, EDA, or predictive modeling (specify the who)

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
section: How does these all play together?
text:
How does these all play together?

They all have pros/cons, shine in different use cases
Feel free to mix-and-match. Here’s one approach:

Positron Databot for initial exploratory data analysis
Positron Assistant for general day-to-day data science analysis and coding tasks
Claude Code for large-scale code refactoring, package development, documentation, non-data-science tasks
GitHub Copilot for in-line code suggestions, pull requests, commit messages, code reviews, etc

But really, it’s up to you!

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Best Practices

Remain accountable
Verify AI-generated content - keep a human in the loop
Check sources
Cite usage appropriately
Maintain data privacy (as needed)
Stay critical, skeptical, open-minded, and curious

title: AI coding assistants for Positron
text:
Thanks!
This presentation created with Quarto, Positron, and GitHub Copilot
Reach out: Gavin McDonald
gmcdonald@bren.ucsb.edu

title: Exploring and modeling Palmer Penguins bill length
text:
Note



This report was generated using AI under general human direction. At the time of generation, the contents have not been comprehensively reviewed by a human analyst.

title: Exploring and modeling Palmer Penguins bill length
text:
Introduction
The Palmer Penguins dataset contains measurements of 344 penguins from three species (Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo) collected from islands in the Palmer Archipelago, Antarctica. This analysis explores the relationships between bill dimensions and other physical characteristics, then builds predictive models to understand what factors best predict bill length.
Our primary goal is to predict bill length (in millimeters) using other measurements including species, island, bill depth, flipper length, body mass, sex, and year of observation.

title: Exploring and modeling Palmer Penguins bill length
text:
Data exploration

Load packages and data

library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.1.0     


── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(tidymodels)

── Attaching packages ────────────────────────────────────── tidymodels 1.4.1 ──
✔ broom        1.0.10     ✔ rsample      1.3.1 
✔ dials        1.4.2      ✔ tailor       0.1.0 
✔ infer        1.0.9      ✔ tune         2.0.0 
✔ modeldata    1.5.1      ✔ workflows    1.3.0 
✔ parsnip      1.3.3      ✔ workflowsets 1.1.1 
✔ recipes      1.3.1      ✔ yardstick    1.3.2 
── Conflicts ───────────────────────────────────────── tidymodels_conflicts() ──
✖ scales::discard() masks purrr::discard()
✖ dplyr::filter()   masks stats::filter()
✖ recipes::fixed()  masks stringr::fixed()
✖ dplyr::lag()      masks stats::lag()
✖ yardstick::spec() masks readr::spec()
✖ recipes::step()   masks stats::step()

library(ranger)
library(patchwork)


# The penguins dataset comes from the palmerpenguins package
data(penguins)



Dataset overview

# Display the dataset structure
glimpse(penguins)

Rows: 344
Columns: 7
$ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
$ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
$ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, NA, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 34.1, …
$ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, NA, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 18.1, …
$ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, NA, 193, 190, 181, 195, 193, 190, 186…
$ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, NA, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3475, …
$ sex               <fct> male, female, female, NA, female, male, female, male…


The dataset contains 344 penguins with 7 variables. Let’s check for missing values:

# Check for missing values per column
penguins |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "missing_count"
  ) |>
  filter(missing_count > 0)

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  variable          missing_count
  <chr>                     <int>
1 bill_length_mm                2
2 bill_depth_mm                 2
3 flipper_length_mm             2
4 body_mass_g                   2
5 sex                          11



# Summary statistics
summary(penguins)

      species          island    bill_length_mm  bill_depth_mm  
 Adelie   :152   Biscoe   :168   Min.   :32.10   Min.   :13.10  
 Chinstrap: 68   Dream    :124   1st Qu.:39.23   1st Qu.:15.60  
 Gentoo   :124   Torgersen: 52   Median :44.45   Median :17.30  
                                 Mean   :43.92   Mean   :17.15  
                                 3rd Qu.:48.50   3rd Qu.:18.70  
                                 Max.   :59.60   Max.   :21.50  
                                 NA's   :2       NA's   :2      
 flipper_length_mm  body_mass_g       sex     
 Min.   :172.0     Min.   :2700   female:165  
 1st Qu.:190.0     1st Qu.:3550   male  :168  
 Median :197.0     Median :4050   NA's  : 11  
 Mean   :200.9     Mean   :4202               
 3rd Qu.:213.0     3rd Qu.:4750               
 Max.   :231.0     Max.   :6300               
 NA's   :2         NA's   :2                  




Bill dimensions by species
One of the most striking patterns in the data is how bill dimensions vary by species. Let’s visualize these relationships:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill dimensions by penguin species",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Bill depth (mm)",
    color = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).








Figure 1: Scatter plot showing the relationship between bill length and bill depth for three penguin species. Each species forms a distinct cluster, suggesting species is a strong predictor of bill dimensions.






# Box plot for bill length
p1 <- ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill length by species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Bill length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Box plot for bill depth
p2 <- ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = bill_depth_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill depth by species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Bill depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Combine plots
p1 + p2

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).
Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).








Figure 2: Box plots showing the distribution of bill length and bill depth across species. Adelie penguins have the shortest but deepest bills, while Chinstrap penguins have the longest bills.






# Density plot for bill length
d1 <- ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of bill length",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# Density plot for bill depth
d2 <- ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_depth_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of bill depth",
    x = "Bill depth (mm)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# Combine plots
d1 + d2 + plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).
Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).








Figure 3: Density plots showing the distribution of bill dimensions across species. The separation between species is clear, particularly for bill depth where Gentoo penguins are distinctly different.





Key observations:

Adelie penguins have the shortest bills (~36-42 mm) but the deepest (~17-19 mm)
Chinstrap penguins have the longest bills (~46-52 mm) and moderately deep bills (~17-19 mm)
Gentoo penguins have intermediate length bills (~45-50 mm) but the shallowest (~14-16 mm)
The three species form distinct clusters in the bill dimension space

title: Exploring and modeling Palmer Penguins bill length
text:
Predictive modeling

Data preparation
We’ll build models to predict bill length using tidymodels. First, we need to remove rows with missing bill length values and split the data:

# Remove rows with missing bill_length_mm
penguins_clean <- penguins |>
  filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm))

# Split the data with stratification
set.seed(149)
split <- initial_split(penguins_clean, strata = bill_length_mm)
train <- training(split)
test <- testing(split)

# Create 10-fold cross-validation
set.seed(234)
folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 10, strata = bill_length_mm)

# Define metrics for regression
metrics <- metric_set(rmse, rsq)

We have 255 observations in the training set and 87 in the test set.


Model 1: Random forest
We’ll start with a random forest model using the ranger engine. The recipe handles missing values in predictors and creates dummy variables for categorical predictors:

# Create a recipe with preprocessing
rec_rf <- recipe(bill_length_mm ~ ., data = train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

# Define the random forest model specification
spec_rf <- rand_forest(mode = "regression") |>
  set_engine("ranger")

# Create workflow
wf_rf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_rf) |>
  add_model(spec_rf)

# Fit on cross-validation folds
results_rf <- fit_resamples(wf_rf, resamples = folds, metrics = metrics)

# Show results
collect_metrics(results_rf)

# A tibble: 2 × 6
  .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 rmse    standard   2.44     10  0.149  pre0_mod0_post0
2 rsq     standard   0.818    10  0.0143 pre0_mod0_post0


The random forest model achieves an RMSE of approximately 2.44 mm and an R² of 0.818.


Variable importance
To understand which predictors are most important, we can examine variable importance from the random forest:

# Refit with importance calculation
spec_rf_imp <- rand_forest(mode = "regression") |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity")

wf_rf_imp <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_rf) |>
  add_model(spec_rf_imp)

rf_fit_imp <- fit(wf_rf_imp, data = train)

# Extract variable importance
rf_model_imp <- extract_fit_parsnip(rf_fit_imp)
importance <- rf_model_imp$fit$variable.importance

# Convert to data frame and sort
importance_df <- data.frame(
  variable = names(importance),
  importance = importance
) |>
  arrange(desc(importance))

importance_df

                           variable importance
species_Chinstrap species_Chinstrap  1645.0930
flipper_length_mm flipper_length_mm  1612.9706
body_mass_g             body_mass_g  1113.5200
species_Gentoo       species_Gentoo   757.3179
bill_depth_mm         bill_depth_mm   680.2905
sex_male                   sex_male   277.1095
island_Dream           island_Dream   222.2744
island_Torgersen   island_Torgersen   151.6209



ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, importance), y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variable importance in random forest model",
    subtitle = "Predicting penguin bill length",
    x = "Variable",
    y = "Importance (impurity reduction)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )







Figure 4: Variable importance from the random forest model. Species (particularly Chinstrap) and flipper length are the most important predictors of bill length, followed by body mass and bill depth.





Key findings:

Species (particularly Chinstrap) is the most important predictor
Flipper length is nearly equally important
Body mass and bill depth are also substantial contributors
Island and sex have relatively low importance



Model 2: Linear regression
To test whether the non-linear relationships captured by random forest provide meaningful benefit, let’s compare with a simple linear regression:

# Use the same recipe
rec_lm <- recipe(bill_length_mm ~ ., data = train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

# Define linear regression specification
spec_lm <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm")

# Create workflow
wf_lm <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_lm) |>
  add_model(spec_lm)

# Fit on cross-validation folds
results_lm <- fit_resamples(wf_lm, resamples = folds, metrics = metrics)

# Show results
collect_metrics(results_lm)

# A tibble: 2 × 6
  .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 rmse    standard   2.26     10  0.142  pre0_mod0_post0
2 rsq     standard   0.837    10  0.0161 pre0_mod0_post0




Model comparison

# Create comparison table
model_comparison <- bind_rows(
  collect_metrics(results_rf) |> mutate(model = "Random Forest"),
  collect_metrics(results_lm) |> mutate(model = "Linear Regression")
) |>
  select(model, .metric, mean, std_err) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = c(mean, std_err)) |>
  arrange(mean_rmse)

model_comparison

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  model             mean_rmse mean_rsq std_err_rmse std_err_rsq
  <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
1 Linear Regression      2.26    0.837        0.142      0.0161
2 Random Forest          2.44    0.818        0.149      0.0143


Surprisingly, the linear regression model performs slightly better than the random forest:

Linear Regression: RMSE = 2.26 mm, R² = 0.837
Random Forest: RMSE = 2.44 mm, R² = 0.818

title: Exploring and modeling Palmer Penguins bill length
text:
Findings and conclusions
This analysis of the Palmer Penguins dataset reveals several important insights:

Bill dimensions vary systematically by species: Chinstrap penguins have the longest bills, while Adelie penguins have the deepest bills. These differences are substantial and consistent.
Linear relationships dominate: Despite the random forest’s ability to capture complex non-linear patterns, a simple linear regression model performs slightly better (RMSE of 2.26 mm vs 2.44 mm). This suggests that the relationships between predictors and bill length are largely linear.
Key predictors: Species identity and overall body size (particularly flipper length and body mass) are the strongest predictors of bill length. This makes biological sense - larger penguins tend to have longer bills, and different species have characteristically different bill shapes.
Model accuracy: Both models achieve strong predictive performance, explaining over 80% of the variation in bill length with an error of approximately 2-2.5 mm. This level of accuracy could be useful for estimating bill length from other measurements when direct measurement is difficult.
Practical implications: The success of the linear model suggests that simple, interpretable models may be sufficient for many biological prediction tasks, even when more complex machine learning methods are available.

Future work could explore:

Testing the final linear model on the held-out test set
Examining residual patterns to identify any systematic prediction errors
Building separate models for each species to capture species-specific relationships
Investigating whether interaction terms improve model performance
